speaker: interviewer
text: --New York. It's May 15, 1996. Dr. Messinger, did you ever have an opportunity to have a reunion with any of the passengers from the

speaker: interviewee
text: Two. Two reunions. One was, uh, the 50th anniversary of the-- the, uh, trip in 1989. We had a reunion in Chicago. And, uh, actually, I think it was one of the universities there. I believe it was Loyola. Through a grant, they did interviews of us and, uh, produced a tape of those interviews. It was very interesting to-- to meet some of these people. I didn't remember any of them, of course. But it was-- it was fun meeting them and exchanging stories, and so on. But what was even better was, um, two years ago, in May of 1994, we did-- there was a, um-- a group in Canada that did a documentary. And they did it on the What they-- uh, they found a lot of survivors. They interviewed us beforehand. And then they picked eight to be on-- in this, uh, documentary. Uh, what we did was we went on a-- on a cruise ship out of Miami, and they filmed the-- this documentary on the cruise ship. We were sailing along the northern coast of Florida exactly whatever number of years-- 55 years after-- exactly 55 years after we had, uh, arrived in Havana in 1939. So it was-- it was very emotional for us. But what happened-- this was a whole week of, uh-- of filming this documentary. And I have the documentary, by the way. They-- it was produced, uh, with funds from the Canadian, French, and German governments. And this documentary was shown in those countries. As a matter of fact, it was shown in Toronto. And I have a lot of friends there, and they saw it. And-- uh, so-- but it was never shown in the United States. Which is too bad, because I thought it was a good documentary. But I met these other survivors from the Uh, three ladies came from England, and the rest were from different cities in the United States. And, um, it was-- it was very interesting doing this documentary, talking about our experiences and feelings, especially privately-- not while we were doing the documentary, but just sitting around and talking. And for me, it was sort of like a catharsis. I-- I almost didn't go on this, because I-- after my, uh, experiences on the and on the which finally brought us to the United States, I never wanted to be on another ship. In fact, I had this-- I think it was subconscious, really. I really didn't want to go on the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when this-- uh, they said they were going to do this documentary on a ship, I-- my first reaction was to say, no, I'm not going to-- I'm not going to do it. But then I thought to myself, you know, this is really an important thing to do. And I should do it, and I shouldn't allow my-- my fear of ships to stand in the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the things that, uh, this trip did was it did away with this fear. I-- in that way, it was a catharsis. And also, [SIGHS] it allowed me to look at my experiences, um, in a slightly different way. Through-- you know, through the stories of other people and what happened to them. I could sort of compare what happened to them to what happened to me. For instance, two sisters from England came to this, uh, documentary filming. And, um, their father was also Polish. And they had lived in Berlin. And their father was also picked up on October 28th and shipped back to Poland, just like my father and my cousin Edith's father, which were shipped back to Poland. They, uh, got on the but their father could not get a transit visa into Germany. My father did. Their father died in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, you know, I-- in that sense, it was-- it was wonderful, because I-- I realized that many of the things that-- that were bothering me about, uh-- about, you know, a lot of these things that happened to me, were not as bad as they might have been. I just got a lot more insight into-- into what had happened. So the-- those reunions were really great things for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do want to say one other thing. Um-- there's-- I guess the story of the really, uh, has a statement to make. And that statement is that although the Germans did the actual killing, there were a lot of other countries and a lot of other people who, although they didn't kill, passively, uh, allowed this killing to happen. I mean, you know, here we were on this ship. Cuba wouldn't let us in. We begged the United States, Canada, other countries in the Western hemisphere to let us in. They wouldn't let us in. I mean, the fact is that most of the people who were on that ship, especially those who ended up in Holland, Belgium, and France-- most of those people were killed in the concentration camps. They were picked up by the Germans. And-- and they could have-- I mean, it would have been so easy. So easy. It's hard to-- to-- I mean, you know, look-- I'm-- I'm really an American, and I appreciate the United States probably more than most people who are born here. Because I-- I've had all these experiences in other countries. But it's-- it's difficult to give the United States for not allowing us in at that time. I mean, you know, the United States is basically a country of-- of immigrants. Everybody is an immigrant except the American Indians. And it was-- the United States was not true to its history when it-- it didn't allow us in. Historically, everybody was a-- an immigrant. And we were just other immigrants that were trying to-- to get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people in the United States think was going to happen if they let another 900 people in? Nothing. Nothing. Or even a few million.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I understand that there were political problems. That there was the Depression going on at that time. But still, it-- it was-- it was not right. It was not right, what the United States did. Canada, the same. And the other-- some of the other countries also. So, um, as I said, the Germans did the killing. But other-- other, uh, people in countries helped unwittingly or-- or passively.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for giving your-- your story to us. We appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I, uh-- I wanted to introduce some of the relatives who were part of this story. My mother Paula is, uh, in a home. She's still alive. She's going to be 91 in a few days. And, uh-- but she's got Alzheimer's. And, uh, my Aunt Rose, who was in Havana at the time the ship arrived, as well as my aunt Frieda, and my Uncle Al, and my Uncle Louie-- they're all dead. Uh, Rose is still alive. But she's in a home too, and it's difficult for her to come. Uh, Edith, my cousin who was in Havana-- that's Edith Lieberman. She later married, uh, Jerry Cohen, so she's Edith Lieberman-Cohen. She was in Havana when our ship arrived. She was one of the people who I was yelling at in those fishing boats, uh, that were circling around the Unfortunately, she died three years ago, so she can't be here. But, uh, I-- I wanted at least part of the family to be here. So Edith's son Larry is here, Larry Cohen. And, uh, I don't know if you want to say something, but you can.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I'm fortunate to have heard stories from my mother and from Sol, um, recounting what had happened. So I guess I'm fortunate in that I had some-- some experience-- um, firsthand experience in hearing what has happened. Which a lot of people my age really haven't. And, um, hopefully, we'll just be able to pass this onto our children and let them know, um, the travesties that occurred. And, um, hopefully, a documentary such as this will help preserve that. Because it shouldn't be forgotten.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do-- uh, I'm glad you-- you said that. I do give talks to-- especially to, uh, schoolkids-- about my experience. And I've done it many times. And, uh, I think it's important for-- for people to know what happened. Especially in view of the fact that there are people going around today saying that none of this ever happened, you know. It's not all a lie-- it's not true. Uh, so, I, uh-- I do that. And, uh-- and I will continue. I-- it's not the most pleasant thing. I mean, you know, I'd just as soon not think about it. But-- but I think it's very important. Uh, and, of course, Larry-- [LAUGHS] he's heard it many times, probably too often. But, uh, it's-- you know, it's part of our family's, uh, life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, the picture you see are my, uh, paternal grandparents. Um, it was taken-- this picture was taken in 1935 in Krakow at, uh, my aunt's wedding-- that's my father's sister's wedding. Ida was her name. She married, uh, Henrik Rakowsky. They had, uh, a boy and then twins. All of them, including my grandparents, who are in this picture, were killed in the Holocaust in Poland. Uh, the two people in the center are my maternal great-grandparents. Uh, on the left is my mother when she was in her early 20s. And on the right is her, uh, younger brother Aaron. Um, Aaron died, uh, in Poland during the Holocaust. My mother is still alive. And my great-grandmother died in Vienna in the 1930s. My great-grandfather came to the United States in 1938 and died in New York a few years later. Uh, this is my birth certificate, which says that I was born in June 16, 1932. Of course, it's in German, because I was born in Germany. It says [GERMAN] And, uh, it has all the information on there. Uh, the best picture of my mother and father and myself when I was, I guess maybe two or three. So this must have been taken in 1934 or '35. This was taken in Berlin. Uh, this picture was taken, I think, around 1937, maybe 1938-- no, 1937. Uh, we went to the, uh, North Sea on-- on a vacation, the whole family. And, uh, we were in this little boat. Uh, now-- the three on the right-- I'm sorry, the three on the left are myself, my father, and his sister, my Aunt Rose. In the center is my uncle [? Alan, ?] or, as he was called, Alter, and his daughter Sylvia. And on the right-- uh, starting at the far right is my aunt Frieda, my father's sister, her daughter Edith. Both of them and my Aunt Rose, uh, who is on the left side of the picture, were in Cuba when, uh, the arrived. And finally, um, the, uh-- the third person on the right side of the picture is my mother. Uh, this is a picture of my mother and me boarding the on, uh, May 14th, 1939. We're the last two, uh, people on the right in the-- in the picture, on the gangplank. Uh, this is my mother's passport. And I was on her passport. You can see my name, uh, near the bottom on the, uh, left-hand page. It's a Polish passport. Uh, this is inside my mother's passport. And it shows the stamp which was put in on the day that we boarded the the 13th of May, 1939. The ship left the following day. And, uh, this is the stamp inside my mother's visa. Which was put on when we landed in Antwerp on June 17th, 1939. Uh, this is on board the You can see the name of the on the lifeboat in the background. Uh, my father, my mother, and I are on the extreme right of the picture. And, uh, on the left side, wearing a cap and the lady with the kerchief on her head, uh, next to him, holding that big bag, is, uh, my Aunt Rose's-- are my Aunt Rose's in-laws, Mr. and Mrs. Meyer. They were on the same ship with us. This is another picture of, uh, my parents and me on the, uh, Uh, this is a picture of my father and me on the deck of the You can see the, uh, deck chairs in the background. And I had, uh-- my parents had bought me a new watch, I guess, before we left Germany. My mother had, anyway. And I'm showing it off. Uh, this is a picture of the, uh, baggage declaration of the the boat that we finally, uh, came to the United States on in 1942. And, uh, my father's name is on the bottom at the right. Uh, this is an affidavit made by Louis Maisel in 1940, and sent to us to, uh, help us in our quest for getting a visa to the United States. Um, Louis Masel was a relative of a relative and, uh, helped us out in this way. Uh, this is a picture of my mother taken in Buffalo about four years ago. That would be about 1992. I guess you could say that, uh, she is the heroine of the story that I just told you.
